questions.passage:
In 1908 the Viennese architect Adolf Loos wrote a lecture with a title that did his arguing for him: 'Ornament and Crime.' Decoration, he announced, was the mark of the primitive and the degenerate; the evolution of culture was synonymous with the removal of ornament from useful objects; the modern man who tattooed himself or gilded his doorframe was a criminal or an atavism. The claim was preposterous, and it conquered the world. Within two generations the architectural profession had internalized Loos so completely that a molded cornice required an apology and a bare concrete plane did not.

Notice what kind of claim it was. Loos did not argue that ornament was ugly, which would have been a judgment of taste, contestable in taste's ordinary courts. He argued that it was wrong: wasteful of labor, dishonest about structure, culturally retrograde. Modernism's founders followed him in converting an aesthetic preference into a morality of surfaces, in which a smooth wall testified to the virtue of its architect and a carved one confessed his corruption. The maneuver was rhetorically brilliant. One can argue with a taste, but a morality puts its opponents in the dock. For most of a century, to defend ornament in a school of architecture was not to be wrong but to be guilty.

What was lost deserves a plain accounting. Ornament was never mere frosting. It was a language, the most democratic one architecture has possessed. The acanthus scroll, the rosette, the checkerboard band of brick: these were legible to the people who walked past them, connecting a new building to older ones and to motifs that circulated through furniture, textiles, and gravestones. Ornament was also employment and transmission, the livelihood of carvers, plasterers, and masons, and the vehicle by which their skills passed between generations. When the profession abolished ornament, it did not liberate buildings from frivolity; it dismissed an entire workforce and burned the dictionary of the one visual language its public actually spoke.

And the smooth surfaces that replaced it were never as innocent as their theology claimed. Austerity is expensive. A wall with moldings forgives an uneven join; a flawless plane of glass or plaster forgives nothing, and demands correspondingly costly precision. The unornamented villa became a signal legible chiefly to other initiates: a display of restraint that functioned exactly as display, minimalism as the most exclusive luxury of all. The doctrine that denounced decoration as waste produced some of the most wasteful buildings in history, and called the result honesty.

It will be objected that the postmodernists already staged this revolt, bolting broken pediments onto office towers in the 1980s, and that the results embarrassed everyone. So they did. But the failure of that episode is evidence about irony, not about ornament. The postmodernists quoted decoration as a joke, in materials that mocked the motifs they borrowed; a styrofoam keystone is not a recovery of the mason's language but a further insult to it. That their sarcasm fell flat tells us nothing about whether the sincere article could live again.

I am aware that no one can simply legislate the acanthus back onto the street. Craft traditions, once broken, are rebuilt slowly, and a revived ornament that ignored a century of construction would be costume. But the first step is available to anyone, and it is intellectual: stop granting the bare wall its unearned moral prestige. A century of building has tested the proposition that people flourish among surfaces stripped of articulation, and the people have returned their verdict in the neighborhoods they pay premiums to inhabit and the ones they cross the street to avoid. The prosecution of ornament was a false conviction. It is overdue for appeal.
questions.stem: Which of the following best states the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: The postmodern movement of the 1980s demonstrated that ornament can never be convincingly revived.
choices.B: Adolf Loos's lecture succeeded because its aesthetic arguments were more rigorous than those of his opponents.
choices.C: Modernism's case against ornament was a moralized aesthetic preference whose triumph destroyed a valuable communal language and deserves to be overturned.
choices.D: Ornamented buildings are generally less expensive to construct and maintain than unornamented ones and should therefore be preferred on straightforwardly economic grounds.

explanations.A: The passage argues the opposite: the postmodern failure 'is evidence about irony, not about ornament,' leaving open whether the sincere article could live again.
explanations.B: The author calls Loos's claim 'preposterous' and locates its success in rhetorical maneuver, converting taste into morality, rather than in rigor.
explanations.C: Correct. The passage traces the conversion of preference into 'a morality of surfaces,' accounts for what was lost as language, livelihood, and transmission, and closes by calling the prosecution of ornament 'a false conviction... overdue for appeal.'
explanations.D: The cost comparison appears in one paragraph to expose austerity's hidden expense, but the passage's case for ornament rests on legibility, craft, and community, not on cheapness as a general rule.

questions.stem: In the passage, the phrase 'a morality of surfaces' refers to:

choices.A: the belief that a building's exterior surfaces should honestly and legibly express its interior structure.
choices.B: the superficiality of critics who judge architecture without technical training.
choices.C: the ethical codes governing relations between architects and their clients.
choices.D: the modernist transformation of a preference about decoration into a judgment of virtue and guilt.

explanations.A: Structural honesty is one charge Loos leveled at ornament, but the phrase names something larger: the whole conversion of the decoration debate into ethics, of which the honesty claim is a single ingredient.
explanations.B: The phrase describes the doctrine's content, not its critics; no untrained critics appear anywhere in the passage.
explanations.C: Professional ethics between architects and clients are never discussed; the morality at issue attaches to walls, not to business conduct.
explanations.D: Correct. The surrounding sentences define the phrase directly: modernists converted 'an aesthetic preference into a morality' under which 'a smooth wall testified to the virtue of its architect and a carved one confessed his corruption,' putting defenders of ornament 'in the dock.'

questions.stem: The author opens with Loos's 'Ornament and Crime' primarily in order to:

choices.A: credit the essay with anticipating the economics of modern construction.
choices.B: identify the founding document of the moralized case against ornament that the passage sets out to overturn.
choices.C: illustrate the intellectual climate of Vienna at the turn of the century.
choices.D: show that even celebrated architectural manifestos rarely exert lasting influence on ordinary professional practice.

explanations.A: The passage's economic point runs against Loos, arguing that his doctrine of anti-waste produced wastefully precise buildings; the essay is given no credit for economic foresight.
explanations.B: Correct. The lecture is introduced as the argument whose 'title did his arguing for him,' whose claim 'conquered the world,' and whose moralizing maneuver the rest of the passage anatomizes and finally calls a false conviction due for appeal.
explanations.C: Vienna appears only as Loos's address; the passage develops the essay's doctrine and consequences, not the city's intellectual scene.
explanations.D: The passage asserts precisely the opposite influence: within two generations the profession had internalized Loos so completely that a cornice required an apology.

questions.stem: Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the author's claim that architectural austerity functioned as an exclusive display of luxury?

choices.A: Several of the most celebrated modernist villas dramatically exceeded their construction budgets because of the exacting precision their smooth surfaces required.
choices.B: Loos himself designed richly paneled interiors for some of his private clients.
choices.C: Postmodern architects of the 1980s came disproportionately from wealthy backgrounds.
choices.D: Construction records show that plain, unornamented building was consistently the cheapest option and was adopted most widely in low-cost mass housing.

explanations.A: Budget overruns driven by demanding precision are the very evidence the author cites for austerity's hidden expense, so this finding would reinforce his claim rather than weaken it.
explanations.B: Loos's private inconsistencies would embarrass the doctrine's founder without touching the economic and social claim about what smooth surfaces cost and signaled.
explanations.C: The backgrounds of postmodern architects concern the ironic revival the author already dismisses, not the status function he attributes to modernist plainness.
explanations.D: Correct. The author's argument holds that flawless planes 'demand correspondingly costly precision' and so served as restraint legible chiefly to initiates; records showing plainness was reliably the cheapest choice and dominated mass housing would contradict both the cost premise and the exclusivity conclusion.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward Loos can best be described as:

choices.A: reverent toward a thinker whose insights remain underappreciated.
choices.B: indifferent to a figure he considers historically marginal.
choices.C: derisive toward his doctrine while acknowledging the potency of his rhetoric.
choices.D: broadly sympathetic to his reforming aims but seriously doubtful about the methods he chose to pursue them.

explanations.A: The author calls the central claim 'preposterous' and spends the passage arguing that its triumph destroyed something valuable, the reverse of reverence.
explanations.B: A figure whose lecture 'conquered the world' and reshaped the profession for a century is treated as anything but marginal.
explanations.C: Correct. The verdict 'preposterous, and it conquered the world' compresses the attitude exactly: contempt for the doctrine's content joined to a clear-eyed account of why its moralizing maneuver was 'rhetorically brilliant.'
explanations.D: The author shares none of Loos's aims; the objection is to the goal of stripping ornament itself, not merely to how it was pursued.

questions.stem: It can most reasonably be inferred that the author would view a contemporary revival of ornament favorably only if it:

choices.A: grew from rebuilt craft traditions and engaged sincerely with present-day construction rather than quoting old motifs ironically.
choices.B: reproduced historical motifs exactly as they appeared before 1908.
choices.C: confined decoration to interiors, leaving street facades unarticulated.
choices.D: was formally mandated by professional architecture schools as a compulsory curricular corrective to a century of orthodox modernist teaching.

explanations.A: Correct. The author dismisses the styrofoam keystone as 'a further insult' to the mason's language, warns that a revival ignoring 'a century of construction would be costume,' and notes that craft traditions 'are rebuilt slowly,' which together define sincerity and craft engagement as his conditions.
explanations.B: Exact reproduction of pre-1908 motifs is what the author explicitly rejects as costume, since it would ignore how building has changed.
explanations.C: The passage prizes ornament precisely for its public legibility to 'the people who walked past,' so banishing it from facades would defeat the point.
explanations.D: The author asks for the withdrawal of unearned moral prestige, an intellectual first step; a new mandate would simply reverse the moralizing coercion he condemns.
